question: How do I join the Office Hours or live workshop if the Zoom link isn’t shared with students?
answer_llm:
The Zoom link is only shared with instructors/presenters/TAs, not students.

If you’re a student, join the Office Hours or live workshop via:
- **YouTube Live** on the DataTalksClub YouTube channel
- **Slido** for questions, with the link pinned in the live chat
- Check the **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** for the video URL before the session starts

Don’t post questions in the chat, since they may be missed if the room is busy.

The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

Students participate via YouTube Live and submit questions to Slido (link is pinned in the chat when live). The video URL should be posted in the [announcements channel on Telegram and Slack](https://t.me/dezoomcamp) before it begins. You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: Where do I begin if I want to take this course from scratch, and what are the main resources I should check first?
answer_llm:
Start with these three resources:

1. **LLM Zoomcamp docs**: https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/  
2. **General Zoomcamp logistics docs**: https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/  
3. **LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository**: https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp

A simple workflow from scratch is:

1. **Watch the lesson videos**
2. **Go through the lesson notebooks/code**
3. **Read the homework instructions on GitHub**
4. **Submit your answers on the course platform before the deadline**

Also, for the lesson materials, use the **GitHub repo as the main entry point**, because it’s the easiest place to stay updated.

question: I can’t find myself on the leaderboard — how do I locate my entry?
answer_llm:
Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — your account was given a random display name when it was created, so that link will take you directly to your entry.

If you want to check the name shown publicly, use **“Edit Course Profile.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Can I take the course at my own pace and still get a certificate at the end?
answer_llm: No. You can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a live cohort. Self-paced mode does not award certificates.
answer_orig:
No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

We don't award certificates for the self-paced mode. The reason is you need to peer-review 3 capstone(s) after submitting your project.

You can only peer-review projects at the time the course is running; after the form is closed and the peer-review list is compiled.

question: Why does the homework keep changing before it’s published?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it’s not ready yet. Until the homework is officially ready, the content can still change.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does **not** contain `[DRAFT]`.

So if it keeps changing before publication, that’s expected. Working on it early is at your own risk because the final version may be different.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why don’t we use LangChain in this course?
answer_llm:
We don’t use LangChain in this course because we want you to build the core RAG pieces yourself first: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

The idea is similar to learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using a framework like React or Angular. Once you understand the fundamentals, frameworks are much easier to use well.

LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error saying insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You’re getting that error because your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough quota/credits to make the request.

There’s no free usage of the OpenAI API, so you need to:

1. Add credits / enable pay-as-you-go in your OpenAI account.
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account in `responses.create(...)`.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you still see the issue after that, I don't know.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does it mean if responses.create fails with an Insufficient credits message?
answer_llm: It means your OpenAI account or key does not have enough credits/quota to complete the request.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: How can I fix the OpenAI API error about not having enough credits for responses.create?
answer_llm:
The error usually means your OpenAI account has no remaining API quota/credits.

Fix:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account here: https://platform.openai.com/settings/organization/billing/overview  
   - Minimum is typically $5.
2. Make sure you’re using a model available on your account in `responses.create(model='gpt-4o', ...)`.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

After that, rerun your command.

If you still see the error, it’s likely still a quota/billing issue.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: Is there a reason the OpenAI responses.create command stops with an insufficient credits error?
answer_llm:
Yes. According to the context, that error usually means your OpenAI account has **insufficient API credits/quota**. There’s no free usage of the OpenAI API, so `responses.create` can stop with an error like:

```text
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: Why am I getting RateLimitError 429 from OpenAI when I run the RAG code, and how do I fix it?
answer_llm:
You’re getting `RateLimitError: 429` because, in this course context, it often means **insufficient quota/credits** on your OpenAI account — not just “too many requests.”

### How to fix it
1. **Add billing credits** to your OpenAI account  
   - Go to: https://platform.openai.com/settings/organization/billing/overview  
   - Add at least **$5**.

2. **Use a model available to your account**  
   In `responses.create(model='gpt-4o', ...)`, make sure the model is one you actually have access to.

3. **Regenerate your API key** after adding credits  
   - Then update the key in your local environment.

### If you’re hitting actual rate limits during the notebook
- Use retries/backoff:
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- Reduce concurrency
- Cache embeddings and responses
- Batch requests where possible
- Use a cheaper model like `gpt-4o-mini`

If the error says `insufficient_quota`, it’s a billing issue.

question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv?
answer_llm:
The course uses **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an all-in-one tool that can replace **pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine**, so you can manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies with one tool and reproducible lockfiles.

You **don’t have to use uv** — **pip and virtualenv still work fine** — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.
answer_orig:

It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file without pushing them to GitHub?
answer_llm:
Put your keys in a local `.env` file, load them with `python-dotenv`, and make sure `.env` is ignored by Git.

1. Create `.env`:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

2. Add this to `.gitignore`:
```gitignore
.env
```

3. Install and use `python-dotenv`:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

If you want a cleaner workflow, you can also use `.env.example` in the repo with only variable names, and keep your real `.env` file out of GitHub.

question: How can I plug Google Gemini into the Module 1 notebook if I want to use the OpenAI-style endpoint instead of the usual setup?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK with Google’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

Set up your client like this:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)
```

Then call Gemini through `chat.completions` and pass your model name, for example:

```python
def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    messages = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=messages
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You’ll need to adjust the notebook code in a few places because this uses the older chat-completions style rather than the newer Responses API, especially for reading outputs and handling tools/function calls.

question: Why does the notebook code need changes when I use Gemini with chat.completions instead of the Responses API?
answer_llm:
Because the notebook examples are built around the OpenAI **Responses API** (`client.responses.create(...)`), but Gemini via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint works better with **chat completions** (`client.chat.completions.create(...)`).

So you need changes in a few places:

- **How you read the answer**
  - Responses API: `response.output`
  - Chat completions: `response.choices[0].message.content`

- **How you handle tools/function calls**
  - The tool schema and tool-call format may differ, so it often needs adaptation for Gemini/provider compatibility.

- **Structured output**
  - If you want parsed Pydantic output, use `chat.completions.parse(...)` instead of `responses.parse(...)`.

In short: the notebook code changes because **Gemini does not implement OpenAI’s Responses API exactly**, even though it supports the OpenAI-compatible chat completions interface.

question: How do I get Pydantic parsed output from Gemini if `responses.parse` isn’t available on the OpenAI-compatible endpoint?
answer_llm:
Use `client.chat.completions.parse(...)` instead of `responses.parse`.

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This gives you a Pydantic object directly, without manual JSON parsing.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

```python
    answer: str
```

```python
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama, visit **https://ollama.com/download** and choose your operating system:

- **macOS**: download the `.pkg` file and install it.
- **Windows**: download the `.msi` file and install it.
- **Linux**: run this command in the terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

And to check the local server:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

  ```bash

  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:


- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.


You should receive a response similar to:


Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

```python
import ollama

response = ollama.chat(
    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why do I get a different token count from tiktoken than the OpenAI API shows?
answer_llm:
Because `tiktoken` only counts the text you pass in, while the OpenAI API also counts hidden chat-formatting tokens.

In particular:

- Each chat message adds about **4 structural tokens**.
- The API adds **2 extra tokens** to mark the start of assistant generation.
- Whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can also change the count slightly.

So your visible prompt might be 320 tokens in `tiktoken`, but OpenAI may report 327 because of those internal additions.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: Is it normal for my prompt to be 320 tokens in tiktoken but 327 in OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Yes — that’s normal.

A `tiktoken` count of 320 vs. OpenAI reporting 327 can happen because OpenAI adds internal chat-formatting tokens that aren’t part of your visible prompt text, such as:

- per-message structural tokens
- tokens marking the start of the assistant response
- possible extra tokens from whitespace or Unicode

So a small difference like 7 tokens is expected.

question: How can I keep my API key loaded automatically so I don’t have to export it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use `dirdotenv` so the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then in your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the API key will be loaded automatically in new terminals when you enter the project directory.

If you already use it, `direnv` also works.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```


After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide on field weights for minsearch without just guessing?
answer_llm:
Use a systematic evaluation, not guessing:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights for minsearch.
4. Compare results with retrieval metrics like hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR.

You can do trial and error for small projects, but evaluation against a ground-truth dataset is the more reliable approach.

question: What’s the best way to clean up documents before using them in RAG, especially when there’s HTML, duplicates, or OCR noise?
answer_llm:
The best approach is to **clean the documents before chunking/indexing** so retrieval works on useful text, not noise.

From the course guidance, the main cleanup steps are:

- **Remove obvious noise**:
  - broken HTML
  - duplicate text
  - boilerplate
  - OCR errors / OCR noise
  - repeated headers and footers
- **Preserve useful structure**:
  - titles
  - section names
  - dates
  - page numbers
  - speaker names
  - Q&A structure
- **Store the cleaned output in a structured format** that’s easy to process, often JSON
- **Then chunk the cleaned text**
- **Keep metadata** for filtering/ranking later

For HTML specifically, strip the markup and keep the meaningful text. For duplicates, remove repeated content before indexing. For OCR noise, clean up obvious recognition mistakes and repeated artifacts as much as possible.

The goal is not just shorter text — it’s **more accurate retrieval**.

- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the result in a structured format that is easy to process. JSON is often convenient, but it is not mandatory.
- Chunk the documents in a way that keeps related context together.
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later.

The exact format depends on the source data. The goal is not just to make the text shorter, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, does the "Failed to hardlink files" message from uv mean the install broke?
answer_llm:
No. In GitHub Codespaces, `"Failed to hardlink files"` from `uv` is a warning, not a failure. `uv` falls back to copying the files, so the package still installs.

If you want to suppress it for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: My WSL2 model says it needs more system memory than is available, but my PC has enough RAM — why is that happening?
answer_llm:
Your WSL2 VM is only allowed to use the amount of RAM configured for WSL2, not all the RAM in your PC.

So even if your computer has enough memory overall, WSL2 may still be limited to a smaller value like `Y.Y GiB`, which is why the model reports not enough available system memory.

To fix it, set a larger WSL2 memory limit in `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`, for example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, verify inside WSL with `free`.

If you want, I can also help you choose a good memory value for your machine.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: How do I give WSL2 more RAM so I can run the model without the memory error?
answer_llm:
Your WSL2 is likely limited to using only part of your computer’s RAM. To give it more memory:

1. Create a file named `.wslconfig` in your Windows user profile folder:
   `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

2. Add this content:
   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL:
   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Check the available memory inside WSL with:
   ```bash
   free
   ```

You can change `8GB` to whatever amount you want to allocate.

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: After changing WSL2 memory settings, what do I need to restart or check to make sure the new RAM limit is applied?
answer_llm:
Restart WSL with:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

Then reopen WSL and check the new limit with:

```bash
free
```

to verify the RAM change was applied.

question: My GitHub Codespace keeps hanging on "Finishing up" and never opens — what’s the quickest thing to try first?
answer_llm:
The quickest first thing to try is:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start it again**

If it still hangs on “Finishing up,” try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or use a different browser.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: I stopped my Codespace and now my setup looks wiped — did I lose everything, or does /workspaces usually stay there?
answer_llm:
The repo in `/workspaces` usually **persists across stop/start**. So if you just **stopped** the Codespace, you likely did **not** lose your project files.

What can disappear:
- **System/global installs outside your project** may not survive a rebuild or a brand-new codespace.
- **Uncommitted changes** survive a stop/start, but can be lost if you **delete** or **recreate** the codespace.

If it looks wiped, try:
1. Restarting the codespace
2. Opening it in the **browser** or a different browser
3. If needed, rebuild/recreate it — then reinstall dependencies with `uv sync`

If you want, I can also help you figure out whether you’re seeing a normal rebuild vs. actual data loss.

question: Can I skip OpenAI and use some other LLM API in the RAG module?
answer_llm:
Yes — you can skip OpenAI and use another LLM API in the RAG module.

The course is not tied to OpenAI. You can use other providers such as Groq, OpenRouter, Gemini, DeepSeek, Mistral, Azure OpenAI, or even a local model via Ollama/vLLM/LM Studio, as long as you have an OpenAI-compatible endpoint or adjust the code accordingly.

If you want, I can also tell you which alternatives are easiest to plug into the Module 1 notebook.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop getting 429 errors from OpenAI when my notebook loops over lots of docs?
answer_llm:
Reduce the number of API calls before handling errors:

- **Retry with backoff:** use the OpenAI client’s automatic retries, e.g. `client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)`.
- **Lower concurrency:** keep thread/process pools small, around **2–3 workers**.
- **Cache results:** save embeddings and LLM outputs to disk so reruns don’t call the API again.
- **Batch requests:** send lists of texts in one call instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider:** `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier can help.

If the error is `insufficient_quota`, that’s a billing issue, not just a rate limit.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: Should I increase retries with exponential backoff in the OpenAI client, and how do I do that?
answer_llm:
Yes — the `openai` Python client already retries automatically with exponential backoff, so the usual recommendation is to **increase the retry limit** so transient `429` errors have more chances to clear.

Use:

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

A few notes:
- This helps with **temporary rate limits**.
- If you see `insufficient_quota`, that’s a **billing issue**, not a retryable rate-limit problem.
- It’s also a good idea to **reduce concurrency**, **cache results**, and **batch requests** where possible.

answer_llm: Yes — you can reduce costs by using a cheap model like `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq. The context also recommends pinning dependency versions and documenting setup so reviewers can reproduce the project with their own credentials.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly counts as a tool or function the model can use?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a tool/function is anything the model can call to get external help. Examples include:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key idea is that the model decides when to use the tool/function, instead of retrieval happening as a fixed pre-step like in basic RAG.

In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How does tool use in agentic RAG differ from the fixed retrieval step in basic RAG?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, the model can decide **when to call a tool/function** and which tool to use based on the situation.

In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a **fixed step** that happens **before** the model generates an answer.

So the difference is:

- **Basic RAG:** retrieve first, then answer
- **Agentic RAG:** the model can dynamically choose to retrieve or use other tools during the process

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I try `client.responses.create(...)` with a non-OpenAI model?
answer_llm:
Because `client.responses.create(...)` is using OpenAI’s **Responses API**, which is **only implemented by OpenAI**. With non-OpenAI providers, the returned object doesn’t have the OpenAI Responses structure, so trying to access `response.output` can fail with errors like:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

For non-OpenAI models, use the **chat-completions API** instead and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

Example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # adapt to provider format if needed
)

return response.choices[0].message.content
```

So the fix is: **don’t use `responses.create` with non-OpenAI providers**; switch to `chat.completions.create`.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

Why does toyaikit give a 404 with Groq or another non-OpenAI model, and what should I change to make it work?
answer_llm:
It’s because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **OpenAI-specific**. Groq and other non-OpenAI providers don’t implement it, so you get a 404.

What to change:
- Use the **chat completions** versions instead:
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

If you’re using Groq, also point the OpenAI client at Groq’s OpenAI-compatible base URL:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)

llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    client=groq_client,
)
```

For Anthropic, use `AnthropicClient` with `AnthropicMessagesRunner` instead.

So the short answer is: **switch from Responses API classes to chat-completions classes, and use the provider’s OpenAI-compatible base URL/model name if applicable.**
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Do I need the Responses endpoint for toyaikit, or can I use chat completions with other providers?
answer_llm:
No — you do **not** need the OpenAI Responses endpoint for toyaikit if you’re using a non-OpenAI provider.

Use the **chat completions** classes instead:
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient` + `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner` for OpenAI-compatible providers like Groq
- `AnthropicClient` + `AnthropicMessagesRunner` for Anthropic

The Responses endpoint (`responses.create`) is OpenAI-specific, so providers like Groq, Mistral, Gemini, etc. won’t support it. For those, use `chat.completions.create` instead.

So yes, you can use chat completions with other providers.

Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

```python
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

)

    llm_client=llm_client,
```

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create(), and not a 429 like with OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Because OpenRouter may reject the request **before the model runs** if `responses.create()` doesn’t have a reasonable `max_output_tokens` limit. OpenRouter checks billing/limits against the **maximum possible output** (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can get a **402**.

OpenAI, by contrast, usually returns **429** for insufficient quota/rate-limit situations.

**Fix:** pass a lower `max_output_tokens`, e.g.:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using `rag_helper.py` or ToyAIKit, add the same setting there too.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I’m not using OpenAI?
answer_llm:
For the Module 1 homework, if you’re using a different provider, **get the token count from the model’s response object**, not from `tiktoken`.

- Look for usage fields like `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens` depending on the provider/API style.
- **Do not use** `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, etc., because tokenization differs by model.
- If the provider doesn’t expose token usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.
- For multiple-choice questions, choose the **closest option**.
answer_orig:
For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why does my homework repo link get a 500 or other non-200 error when the checker opens it, and what should I change so it passes?
answer_llm:
The checker is trying to open your repo with a normal GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `500`, `404`, or anything else, it usually means the link isn’t publicly reachable as-is.

What to change:
- Make the repo **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in `.git`
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Check for a **typo** in the URL

Quick test: open the link in a private/incognito browser window. If it works there, it should pass the checker too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: Could the submission be failing because my GitHub repo is private, and how do I verify that's the problem?
answer_llm:
Yes — a private GitHub repo could definitely cause the submission to fail, because the checker expects to fetch your submitted URL and get an HTTP `200` response.

How to verify:
- Open the repo URL in a **private/incognito browser window**
- If it **doesn’t load there**, the checker likely can’t access it either
- If it loads publicly, it should work for the checker

Also make sure:
- You submit the **plain repo URL** like `https://github.com/you/repo`
- Not the `.git` URL
- There’s no typo in the link

If it’s private, make the repository public.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we convert non-numerical data into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are numerical representations of non-numerical data, such as text, that preserve meaning and context. If two inputs are similar, their embeddings should also be numerically close to each other.

We convert non-numerical data into numbers so we can use mathematical methods to compare them, like cosine similarity or dot products. This lets a vector search system find items with similar meaning, not just matching words.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings preserve meaning and context when turning text or other data into numerical form?
answer_llm: Embeddings preserve meaning and context by converting non-numerical data into numerical data in a way that keeps similar inputs close together in the vector space. So if two pieces of text have similar meaning, their embeddings should produce similar numerical values. This proximity can then be used with semantic similarity algorithms.

question: Why should similar inputs produce similar vectors, and how is that used for semantic similarity?
answer_llm:
Similar inputs should produce similar vectors because embeddings are meant to preserve meaning and context: if two pieces of data are alike, their numerical representations should be close in vector space.

That’s what makes semantic similarity possible. Once similar items are near each other as vectors, you can compare them with measures like cosine similarity. If two vectors point in a similar direction, they have high cosine similarity, which indicates the underlying inputs are semantically similar.

- This is closely related to the **vector space model** and **dimensionality reduction**.

question: Why did the lesson use dot product straight away for similarity, but the homework keeps talking about normalization?
answer_llm:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` worked directly because the embeddings were already normalized to unit length, so dot product = cosine similarity.

question: How do you measure how similar two text vectors are with cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are. For two text vectors, you:

1. Compute the dot product of the vectors.
2. Divide by the product of their magnitudes:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\|A\| \|B\|}
\]

If the vectors are already normalized to unit length, then cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: What does the cosine of the angle between two vectors tell you in document search?
answer_llm:
It tells you how similar or aligned the two vectors are. In document search, that means how similar two documents are based on their content.

If the cosine is:
- `1`, the vectors point in the same direction and are very similar
- `0`, they are orthogonal and have no similarity
- `-1`, they point in opposite directions

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into a dot product once the vectors are normalized?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity becomes a dot product when both vectors are normalized because their lengths are 1.

Cosine similarity is defined as:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \, \|y\|}
\]

If `x` and `y` are unit vectors, then \(\|x\| = \|y\| = 1\), so:

\[
\cos(\theta) = x \cdot y
\]

So normalization removes the magnitude terms, and cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: Why can we use matrix multiplication to compare the query embedding with all document embeddings?
answer_llm:
We can use matrix multiplication because each document embedding can be compared to the query embedding with a dot product, and when the vectors are normalized this dot product equals cosine similarity.  

So instead of looping over documents one by one, we stack all document embeddings into a matrix and multiply it by the query vector to get all similarity scores at once, efficiently.

answer_llm: No. If the embeddings and query are unit vectors, cosine similarity does **not** depend on their length, because their lengths are already 1. In that case, cosine similarity is just the **dot product** between them.

question: How does normalizing embeddings let us compute similarities with a single matrix multiplication?
answer_llm:
If all embeddings are normalized to unit length, then cosine similarity becomes just the dot product.

That means for a query vector \(q\) and a matrix of document embeddings \(X\), you can compute all similarities at once with:

\[

\]

Because each row of \(X\) is a document embedding, this single matrix multiplication gives the similarity between the query and every document efficiently.

question: Why do the dot product and the loop version of vector search not match exactly, even though they should do the same thing?
answer_llm:
They don’t match exactly because of floating-point precision.

`X.dot(v_query)` and the loop version compute the same dot products, but they add the numbers in a different order. Floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so you can get tiny differences in the last decimal places.

So the results are effectively the same, just not bit-for-bit identical. Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

question: Why am I getting IndexError: list index out of range when I try to grab the best chunk in vector search?
answer_llm:
The error usually means the number of embeddings you generated does not match the number of document chunks.

Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list, for example:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should match `len(chunks)`.

question: How do I make sure my chunk embeddings match the number of chunks so I don't get this IndexError?
answer_llm:
Make sure you generate embeddings from the **chunk list**, not from the original documents, so the number of embedding vectors matches the number of chunks.

For example:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Here, `len(X)` should equal `len(chunks)`.

If those counts don’t match, you can get an `IndexError: list index out of range` when trying to access the best chunk.

question: Should I be encoding the chunk contents directly before calling embedder.encode_batch?
answer_llm:
Yes — embed the chunk contents directly.

Use the chunk list to build a list of text contents first, then call `embedder.encode_batch` on that list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Do not encode the whole chunk dict or a different field. The number of embeddings should match `len(chunks)`.

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Here, `X.shape[0]` should equal `len(chunks)`.

question: Why is my Module 2 Q2 cosine similarity not matching any answer choice — did I use the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most common issue is using the wrong embedding model.

For Module 2 Homework, you should use the homework’s `Embedder` from `embedder.py`, not `sentence-transformers` or the lesson model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Also make sure you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field, not the filename or the whole dictionary
- comparing it against the query vector from Q1
- using the fact that `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity

If you still get a value that’s slightly off, pick the closest answer choice — small numerical differences can happen.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini API keys for Kestra if it wants secrets from env vars?
answer_llm:
Set both variables **before** starting Kestra:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # for the AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)           # for Kestra flows
```

Then restart Kestra so it picks them up:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In a Kestra flow, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

If you also use OpenAI or Tavily:

```bash
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)
```

If you want, I can also show the `.env` / `docker compose --env-file` way to do this.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help create Kestra flows, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
It usually means Kestra AI Copilot is **not receiving a valid Gemini API key**, so it falls back to that canned response.

**Check first:** whether the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` is set in the shell before you start Kestra.

```bash
echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
```

For Kestra Open Source, the Copilot uses the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY`, **not** the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` used by the flows.

If needed, set it and restart Kestra:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still happens, the key may be missing, mistyped, or rate-limited.

question: Could the Gemini API be rate-limited or exhausted, and how would I know if that’s why the Copilot stopped working?
answer_llm:
Yes — it could be rate-limited or quota-exhausted. In that case, the Copilot may stop responding properly because the Gemini API returns something like a `429 Resource Exhausted` error.

How to tell:
- If you’ve been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota.
- The context says to wait a minute and retry.
- Also confirm the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` is actually set in the shell used to start Kestra (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`), because a missing/mistyped key can look similar.

If you’re asking specifically whether that’s why the Copilot stopped working, the clue in the context is:
- `429 Resource Exhausted` → likely rate/quota limit
- “I can only assist with creating Kestra flows” → usually means the Copilot didn’t get a valid Gemini API key, not necessarily quota exhaustion

So: rate-limit/quota exhaustion is possible, but the specific refusal message usually points more to a missing or invalid `GEMINI_API_KEY`.

question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is invalid or missing even though I exported SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Kestra likely isn’t reading the key you think it is. The most common issue is a corrupted base64 secret: if you encoded the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded value is wrong and auth fails.

Use `echo -n` and quote the variable:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these:

- Restart Kestra after exporting so it picks up the new env vars:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- In your flow, reference it **without** `SECRET_`:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
- Export the variables in the same shell session you use to start Kestra.

A cleaner option is to put the values in a `.env` file and start Kestra with:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could a trailing newline or space break the base64 secret for Kestra, and how do I encode it the right way?
answer_llm:
Yes. A trailing newline or space can corrupt the base64 value and make Kestra read the secret incorrectly.

Encode it like this:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Key points:
- Use `echo -n` so no newline is added.
- Quote the variable: `"$GEMINI_API_KEY"`.

Then restart Kestra so it picks up the new environment variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In flows, reference it without the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

question: What’s the easiest way to load the Gemini and other keys into Kestra using a .env file?
answer_llm:
The easiest way is to put your keys in a `.env` file and pass it to Kestra when you start it:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

For Kestra, remember that it expects secrets as environment variables with the `SECRET_` prefix and base64-encoded values. For Gemini, you typically set both:

```bash
GEMINI_API_KEY=your-gemini-api-key-here
SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then in the flow you reference it like this:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Also make sure `.env` is in `.gitignore` so you never commit your keys.

question: Why not just use a notebook for these AI workflows instead of an orchestrator?
answer_llm:
Because notebooks are great for learning and experimentation, but they’re not ideal for production-style AI workflows.

An orchestrator gives you things like:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliable chaining of tasks

In this course, Kestra is used to show how AI fits into real workflows, not just notebook cells.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: What does Kestra actually add beyond running a Python script once?
answer_llm:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring/observability
- secret management
- chaining tasks reliably

It also lets the course teach AI-specific workflow patterns, like:

- generating flows with an AI Copilot
- grounding responses with RAG
- using agents that choose tools

So the main value is not the script itself, but running it as part of a more reliable, production-style workflow.

question: Do I actually have to use Kestra in LLM Zoomcamp, or is it just for that module?
answer_llm:
No — you don’t have to use Kestra for the whole LLM Zoomcamp.

Kestra is the orchestrator taught in Module 3, so you should use it for that module’s homework and lessons. But for the capstone project, you can use Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or even no orchestrator at all. A plain Python script is enough for the ingestion-pipeline criterion.

So: **Kestra is mainly for Module 3, not a course-wide requirement.**
answer_orig:

For the **capstone project** you are not restricted in technology: you can use Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or no orchestrator at all. A plain Python script that ingests and indexes your data is enough for full points on the ingestion-pipeline criterion (a Jupyter notebook with the same steps is worth 1 point instead of 2). See the [project guidelines](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/project.md) for details.

If you already know Airflow (or another tool) and want to compare, give Kestra a try for the module and then use whatever fits your project best.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else — see the [supported providers list](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra workflow without hand-writing YAML or any code at all?
answer_llm:
Yes. Kestra has no-code options, so you can build a workflow without hand-writing YAML or code:

- **No-code form editor**: creates a flow from a form, not YAML.
- **AI Copilot**: generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can refine it with follow-up prompts.
- **External agent skills** like Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL: generate flows from your editor and push them into Kestra.

So the answer is **yes** — you can avoid hand-writing YAML or code.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: Why do AI tools like ChatGPT make up Kestra YAML fields that aren’t really there, and how can I catch that before it breaks my flow?
answer_llm:
AI tools like ChatGPT can invent Kestra YAML fields because they’re not grounded in the exact plugin schema of your running Kestra version. They generate “plausible” properties that may look right but don’t actually exist.

Examples from the course:
- GCS Upload: invented `bucket` and `name` instead of the real `to`
- BigQuery LoadFromGcs: invented `projectId` / `dataset` / `table` instead of the real `destinationTable`

To catch this before it breaks your flow:
- Cross-check the YAML against the official Kestra plugin docs
- Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current schema
- Validate and test the flow in your Kestra environment
- Remember that plugin properties can differ by version

If you want, share the YAML snippet and I can help verify it.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: Why do people track token usage in Kestra AI workflows?
answer_llm:
People track token usage in Kestra AI workflows because it helps with:

- **Cost control** — LLM providers bill by tokens, so tracking them helps you budget and catch cost spikes.
- **Prompt optimization** — you can see how much each prompt/response costs and reduce unnecessary tokens.
- **Guardrails** — in scheduled or looping workflows, monitoring tokens helps prevent runaway tasks from creating a big bill.

In Kestra, you can surface token counts from the provider response in logs, metrics, or dashboards.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: Why does Docker Desktop say the path /tmp/... is not shared when I start Kestra?
answer_llm:
Docker Desktop says `/tmp/...` is not shared because the Kestra container is trying to mount a host directory under `/tmp`, but Docker Desktop on your machine is not अनुमति to access that path yet.

The fix is to add `/tmp` to Docker Desktop’s file sharing list:

1. Open **Docker Desktop**
2. Click **Settings** (gear icon)
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**
4. Click **+**
5. Add `/tmp` or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`
6. Click **Apply & restart**

Then run Kestra again with:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```
answer_orig:
This error means Docker Desktop isn't allowed to access the host path the container is trying to mount (commonly `/tmp/kestra-wd` when running Kestra). Add the path to Docker Desktop's file sharing list:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: Why am I getting `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value` when I try to parse the LLM output as JSON in the evaluation notebook?
answer_llm:
The LLM output likely isn’t pure JSON. It may be wrapped in a markdown code fence or include extra prose, so `json.loads(response)` fails with `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value`.

To fix it, force structured JSON output and make the prompt explicit:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

And in your prompt, say something like:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

If you want, I can also show how to make the parsing more robust when the model still adds extra text.
answer_orig:
The LLM sometimes wraps the JSON in a markdown code fence or adds prose around it, so `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```
JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

Force JSON output with OpenAI's `response_format`:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also be explicit in the prompt about the expected shape:

```
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
```

```
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
```

question: Why does my Jupyter kernel crash when I try embedding the ground-truth set?
answer_llm:
Your Jupyter kernel is probably crashing because the machine runs out of memory when the embedding model is loaded together with the rest of the notebook state, especially on small-RAM environments like Codespaces defaults or low-end laptops.

Workarounds:
- Use a smaller embedder, like `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim).
- Run the embedding step in a separate Python script from the terminal, then load the saved vectors back into the notebook.
- Use a machine with more RAM or run locally.
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches with `del` and `gc.collect()`.

If you switch models, re-run the evaluation because your hit-rate / MRR will change.

question: Why am I still hitting Gemini limits even after adding a sleep between calls?
answer_llm:
Because `sleep` only reduces the **per-minute** request rate. Gemini free-tier limits are also **per-day**. So even if you space requests out with `time.sleep(4)`, a long `tqdm` loop can still use up the daily quota in a single run.

What you can do:
- use a cheaper/higher-limit model like `gpt-4o-mini`
- use Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`)
- lower thread-pool concurrency to 2–3 workers
- save partial progress and resume after hitting the limit
answer_orig:
Free-tier Gemini limits both per-minute and per-day requests. Adding `time.sleep(4)` only fixes the per-minute side — a long `tqdm` loop can still blow through the per-day quota in one run.

Options when this happens:

- Spend ~$5 on OpenAI and use `gpt-4o-mini`. It's cheap enough to embed/generate the entire ground-truth set and has higher rate limits.
- Use Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) — generous request-per-minute limits.
- Lower concurrency for thread-pool calls. Use a smaller pool size (2–3 workers) instead of pushing the API hard.
- Resume from where you stopped. Save progress periodically (e.g. dump the partial results to a JSONL file) so a hit limit doesn't lose all work.

question: What's the best way to handle rate-limit errors in a thread pool so I don't lose all the progress?
answer_llm:
The best approach is to **lower concurrency and save progress as you go**.

From the course context:

- Use a **small thread pool** — about **2–3 workers** instead of many.
- **Checkpoint partial results periodically** to something like a **JSONL file**.
- If you hit the limit, **resume from where you stopped** rather than rerunning everything.

If you’re using the OpenAI client, it also helps to set retries with backoff:

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

So in practice: **small pool + retries + periodic saves** is the safest way to avoid losing progress.
